title: Radioactive decay: the rate is the slope of the count
scenario: A sample of $N_0 = 1000$ nuclei decays with decay constant $\lambda = 0.5$ s⁻¹. Each nucleus has the same fixed chance of decaying per second, so the number decaying per second is proportional to how many are left: $dN/dt = -\lambda N$. Solving this gives the exponential $N(t) = N_0 e^{-\lambda t}$, and the half-life $t_{1/2} = \ln 2 / \lambda$ falls out of it. Stacked $N$–$t$ over $dN/dt$–$t$, the decay rate is the slope of the count — the same exponential machine as a discharging capacitor, in the nucleus.

assumptions.claim: A large number of identical nuclei, each decaying independently with a constant probability per unit time, so the count can be treated as a smooth continuous $N(t)$.

steps.label: What the algebra-based course hands you (a half-life)
steps.prose: The sample halves every half-life $t_{1/2}$: after one $t_{1/2}$ half is left, after two a quarter, and so on. You are handed the half-life and the decay curve as rules, with no account of *why* decay is exponential.

steps.label: The decay rate is proportional to the number remaining
steps.prose: Each nucleus has the same fixed chance per second of decaying, so the number decaying per second is proportional to how many are left. That is a first-order differential equation — the rate $dN/dt$ depends on $N$ itself.

steps.label: Solving it gives the exponential; the rate is the slope
steps.prose: The solution is an exponential, and the half-life $t_{1/2} = \ln 2/\lambda$ falls out of it. The decay rate is the *slope* of $N(t)$ — the same exponential machine as a discharging capacitor or a body slowing under drag, in the nucleus.

steps.label: Watch the two panels — the lower is the slope of the upper
steps.prose: Drag $\lambda$: a larger decay constant means a shorter half-life and a steeper fall. At every instant the slope of the upper $N$–$t$ curve is the value of the lower $dN/dt$–$t$ curve, and the area under $dN/dt$ is the number that have decayed. The activity a Geiger counter reads is the magnitude $|dN/dt| = \lambda N$.

proof.heading: The decay rate is proportional to the number remaining — and that rate is the slope of $N(t)$, the panel below.
proof.detail: check $dN/dt$ is the slope; check $dN/dt = -\lambda N$; $t_{1/2} = \ln 2/\lambda$; $1/e$ left after $\tau = 1/\lambda$

checks.claim: The decay rate is the slope of $N$: the lower panel is $\dfrac{dN}{dt}$ exactly, the derivative of the curve above.
Each nucleus decays independently, so the rate is proportional to how many remain: $\dfrac{dN}{dt} = -\lambda N$.
The half-life is $t_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{\lambda}$: after it, exactly half remain, $N(t_{1/2}) = N_0/2$.
After one mean lifetime $\tau = 1/\lambda$, a fraction $1/e$ of the sample remains.

misconception.claim: A radioactive sample decays at a steady rate, losing the same number of nuclei every second until it is gone.
misconception.refuted_by: It does not decay at a steady rate. The number lost per second is proportional to how many remain, $dN/dt = -\lambda N$, so the rate is fastest at the start and slows as the sample shrinks — it never quite reaches zero. That is exactly why decay is *exponential* and is described by a constant *half-life* rather than a constant lifetime: every half-life removes half of whatever is left, not a fixed number. Watch the panels: where $N$ is steepest (at the start) the $dN/dt$ curve is largest in magnitude; as $N$ flattens, the rate fades toward zero. The activity a detector reads is $|dN/dt| = \lambda N$.
